title: The 92-Minute Mission
kicker: Operational Review · or002 · Kimi Chief Session 1b4ba2f1 · 20 Jul 2026
subtitle: Chris dictated his delegation method at 10:06 and handed the chief the keys. One builder, one auditor, six steps — all genuinely executed. novakai-docs renders all eight stores read-only, 107 tests green, built five times faster than Friday night's marathon. Then one question from Chris at 12:19 — “did you click on everything?” — found what two verification passes missed. This is the trace of where that requirement lived, shrank, and died.
subMeta: mission_docs-render-stores · chief wire 2.4 MB + builder wire 9.2 MB + tunnel journal · verified against 5ad6bf7 + live :7100

subject.scope: 08:07–12:31 AEST (round 1 only; round 2 out of scope)

footer: Evidence. Chief wire: ~/.kimi-code/sessions/wd_novakai-command_f6218118a6cc/session_1b4ba2f1…/agents/{main,agent-8}/wire.jsonl · Builder wire (Claude format): ~/.claude/projects/-Users-christopherdasca-Programming-novakai-docs/76036b56….jsonl · Tunnel journal: .novakai-command/messages.jsonl

Artifacts: builder plan novakai-docs/docs/plans/2026-07-20-render-stores-plan.md · audit .novakai/work/audit-render-stores-plan.md · chief log entries log_2026-07-20-010…025 · screenshots: chief's own /tmp/docs-*.png, /tmp/v-*.png, builder's ~/.claude/browse/shots/

Verified live 20 Jul 13:30–13:40 AEST: round-1 inspector census via git show 5ad6bf7 (0 HQInspector refs in the 5 new/repointed views; 4 each in Board/Projects); :7100 mutation probe still refused (stores are read-only). Live session wires copied to scratchpad before reading; read-only throughout — no reviewed code, stores, or sessions were modified. All times AEST.

html: As management, the method's first real run worked: the read-back was genuinely judged, the cross-provider audit killed two real defects before a line of code existed, boundaries held under a dirty tree, and the chief verified with its own hands — re-running tests, probing the API, catching a UI bug the builder's method couldn't see. The failure was not a skipped step. It's that the bar inside step 5 shrank every time someone rewrote it — from Chris's “click every control” to “have a look” to “click through every page” to “clicked all 8 tabs.” Two verification passes together clicked exactly one record. Chris's one question at 12:19 did in six minutes what both passes missed — the same asserted-not-measured failure or001 traced on Friday's build chain, reproduced one management layer up, forty-two minutes before or001 was even filed.

k: method green-light → chief's final report (Friday's build: 8 h)

of the 92 spent waiting — 19 API-500 stall + 25 heartbeat tick-wait
live records ever clicked, across both verification passes

label: Timeline · AEST
note: The gold moment is the probe — one question restoring the bar every layer had compressed. The 10:06 dictation to 11:38 close-out is the method's entire first run: 92 minutes.

label: 2 delegations · fix merged 09:08 · chief re-ran the tests itself

what: six stores built, drift fixed, rename out
label: DEC-003 · 4 defects healed · tombstone rule adopted
19 min lost to API 500s · read-back passed
“did you click on everything?” — dead clicks found in 6 min

html: Everything that was specced shipped and works — I re-verified the survivors today: all 8 stores adapt with zero errors, every mutating route still answers stores are read-only, and the tombstones render as an archive strip, exactly as the audit demanded. What Chris's question exposed was not a build defect. Clicking a record — the way he actually reads a log entry or a key result — did nothing in six of seven record views. Confirmed in the round-1 code: HQInspector appears 4× in Board, 4× in Projects, and zero times in the five views this mission created or repointed.

alt: Log entry row before clicking: truncated body text ending in an ellipsis
cap: the chief, during the probe (12:21): a log entry, body truncated at “…” — the full text unreadable anywhere in the UI.

alt: The same log entry row after clicking: identical, nothing opened
cap: the same pixels. No inspector, no selection, no highlight. This is what “clicked all 8 tabs” cannot detect.

alt: Inspector panel open on the Tasks board showing title, status and refs
cap: Tasks/Board — the only mission view wired to the pre-existing inspector. Click a card: title, status, refs. What Chris expected everywhere.

alt: Requests inbox rendering two pending request cards with numbered options
cap: the chief did drive Requests properly at 11:31 — both pending items, options, ref chips, confirmed live. Rendering got deep checks; interaction got none.

label: Root Cause
headline: The verification bar compressed at every rewrite — “click every control” became “have a look”, then “click through every page”, then “clicked all 8 tabs.” Every layer executed its own copy faithfully; the bar itself was lossy-rewritten, Chris's dictation included.
detail: or001 named the mechanism — requirements die by summarization — on Friday's build chain. This run reproduces it on the management chain, in real time, 42 minutes before or001 was filed. Nobody defied the method: the builder did exactly what its stop point said, the chief did exactly what step 5 said. What no layer carried was the bar in Chris's own words: interact with every record the way Chris would. One question at 12:19 restored it, and the dead clicks surfaced in six minutes.

title: Chris's standing rule
body: “Click every control, interacting exactly the way Chris would.” Full strength — but it lives in Claude-side config (CLAUDE.md + a per-prompt hook). The Kimi chief never sees it.

title: The dictation, 10:06
body: Step 5 as spoken: “have a look yourself at what they built to check they delivered what they said.” Look, not click — the first compression happened at the source.

title: The chief's stop point
body: “Browser-verified by you with ~/.claude/browse (click through every page, screenshots).” Page-level. Records never mentioned.

title: The builder's plan
body: Re-strengthened from its own CLAUDE.md: “every store gets driven in a real browser — click through, scroll, read screenshots,” with a 1-hour verify estimate. The strongest downstream copy of the rule.

title: The builder's verify run
body: 2.5 minutes: 8 tab-clicks via JS eval .click() — blind to pointer-blocked elements — and 1 record click, on the only view that had an inspector. Report up: “browser-verified end-to-end.” Literally true.

title: The chief's STEP 5
body: Tests, typecheck, API — all re-run first-hand. Browser: 2 views deep with real pointer clicks (caught the nav clip), 0 records clicked. Transcript sample = grep counts (100 “browse”, 32 “verif”). The tab-level claim was accepted.

html: Second layer, and the reason even honest verification couldn't fail it: the interaction was never in any spec. The plan's view table maps eight stores to view types — there is no “on click” column. The audit produced twelve findings across timestamps, provenance, tombstones, even the nav overflow — zero about what clicking a record should do. Four capable agents across three providers never asked the question, because the spec was view-shaped and Chris's mental spec — Notion-like, click a row, see everything — is written nowhere a builder or a Kimi chief reads. Verification verified the spec, completely. The spec was missing the dimension.

The mission cost Chris nothing after 10:09 — he was interrupted zero times, per his mandate. The chief was hands-on for 24 of 92 minutes across 13 turns; the rest was waiting, and the waiting had exactly two shapes: a 19-minute provider stall (two API 500s killed the builder's first turns; “delivered” ≠ alive) and ~25 minutes of heartbeat tick-wait — the plan, the audit verdict, and the done-report each sat finished until the next 9/14-minute cron fire noticed them (6.8, 5.5 and 12.3 minutes respectively). The build itself took twenty minutes against a nine-to-eleven-hour human-shaped estimate.

note: Shares of the 92-minute mission window (10:06 green-light → 11:38 final report). Categories overlap — the chief works while the builder builds.

label: 19 min — builder dead on arrival, caught via wire silence at 10:27
25 min — three finished handoffs waited for the next cron fire
24 min across 13 turns — judging, feedback, verify, close-out

title: STEP 1 judged for real, not rubber-stamped
body: The chief graded the read-back against five planted patterns — and the builder surfaced a sixth nobody planted: four of the eight stores would render as 100% corrupt lines under the viewer's contract. The chief recognized it as the mission's real landmine, promoted it into the plan, and ruled adapter-at-the-edge with the ten-principles reasoning Chris asked for. This is the confidence check working exactly as dictated.

title: The audit gate paid for itself twice
body: Re-verified in this review: seven tombstone rows truly carry only updated — the planned adapter would have dropped them; and a Finder-launched .app truly never inherits NVK_STORES_DIR — the packaged app would have shipped empty, the exact bug this mission existed to kill. Feedback went to the builder as one consolidated, encouraging message. The builder's own close-out: “your brief + the codex audit did half the work.”

title: The stall was caught by forensics, not luck
body: Messages said “delivered”; the agent was dead — two Anthropic 500s on its first turns. The chief noticed wire silence, read the session's last lines, diagnosed server-side kill, sent one friendly nudge — activity resumed in 26 seconds — and filed learning_verify-wire-after-spawn with evidence, all before the read-back was even due. Terminate-or-revive judgment, working.

title: Boundaries held under a dirty tree
body: Another session's uncommitted work sat in the same repo the whole mission. Nothing was committed, stashed, or clobbered; the one genuinely contested line in electron/main.ts shipped as docs/patches/packaged-storesdir.patch for its owner to apply. The scope fence and the coordination rule both came from the briefs — and both held.

title: The chief's real clicks caught what the builder's synthetic clicks couldn't
body: The builder verified tabs via JS eval .click(), which fires whether or not a human could reach the element. The chief used real pointer clicks, hit a 15-second timeout on OKRs, read the Playwright log — “nav intercepts pointer events” — and diagnosed the overflow the audit had predicted in finding #8. One level deeper and the same instinct would have found the dead records too.

title: Honest all the way up, fast on the turn
body: The final report separated verified-by-me from trusted-from-builder. When the probe landed, the chief didn't defend — it re-verified in six minutes, confirmed the gap in code (HQInspector wired only into Board), filed both bugs as tasks with refs in the same turn, and owned the miss in plain words: “my first pass clicked views, not records.”

title: Two verification passes, one record clicked
body: The builder's browser pass: 2.5 minutes against a planned hour — 8 tab-clicks, 11 screenshots viewed, exactly 1 record opened (the Board, the only view that could answer). The chief's pass: 2 views driven, 0 records. 79 records were live in the stores that morning. Each pass honestly executed its own layer's copy of the bar — that's the finding above. The floor that fixes it costs six minutes; the probe proved it.

title: The interaction model was in nobody's spec
body: Plan: a view-per-store table, no on-click column. Audit: twelve findings, zero interactional. Chief: ruled on adapters and nav, never on “what does clicking a row do.” Chris's Notion-shaped expectation lived only in his head — and in a Claude-side standing rule the Kimi chief never receives. or001's “spec states, not screens” lands here as: spec interactions, not views.

title: The heartbeat is a polling tax
body: Three finished handoffs sat idle waiting for the next tick: plan → audit dispatch 6.8 min, audit → greenlight 5.5 min, done → verify 12.3 min. Plus seven CronCreate / six CronDelete cycles just to keep the prompt's stage current — state living in a cron prompt means every transition is a delete-and-recreate. The tunnel already supports --interrupt; “done” should wake the chief, with the cron as deadman only.

title: Estimates are still human-shaped
body: The plan estimated engineer-hours; the chief relayed “build will take a few hours”; the cron was tuned to “quiet ticks are fine.” The builder read the codebase in ~2 minutes and built in 20. Nobody re-baselined — which is how a 1-hour verify line became 2.5 minutes without anyone noticing the ratio, and how a 14-minute tick got sized for a 20-minute build.

title: The watchdog cried wolf all morning
body: Every team-channel message in the window that wasn't mission traffic was watchdog noise: “gone quiet, worth a look” for agents deliberately benched with no mission, plus stale “Mail trouble” alerts from the pre-fix era. Zero were acted on, correctly. That ratio is how a real stall gets ignored next week.

title: The day's biggest lesson wasn't filed
body: Eleven learnings were filed today, every one evidence-ref'd — the discipline is real. But “verify at record level, not view level” exists only as an apology in chat. The store built specifically so lessons outlive sessions missed the one the owner personally taught at 12:19.

html: The six steps survived contact — keep all of them. These eight bolt onto the method doc; each traces to a specific win or gap above.

title: Carry the bar verbatim
body: Write the verification bar once, in Chris's words — “click every control, open every record, read what you see” — as a named checklist in the method doc, and paste it verbatim into every brief, stop point, and verify prompt. It was paraphrased four times and died by the fourth. or001 proved checklists eclipse prose; this run proved paraphrase eats bars.

title: Chief's STEP 5 floor: one record per view
body: Real pointer clicks (eval-clicks lie about clickability), at the owner's viewport, one record opened in every view, and read what appears. The probe measured the cost: six minutes.

title: Events wake the chief; cron is the deadman
body: Builder “done” and questions go out as --interrupt to the chief's session; the cron survives only as a slow silence-detector. State moves out of cron prompts and into the mission row, ending the delete-recreate churn.
save: Target: −25 min tick-wait per mission at this scale.

title: Wire-check 60 seconds after every spawn
body: learning_verify-wire-after-spawn already exists with evidence — promote it from a learning into the method doc and the spawn recipe. Delivery ≠ alive; two 500s cost 21% of this mission.

title: Re-baseline time to agent speed
body: When the “20–30 min read” lands in 2 minutes, every downstream estimate is off by an order of magnitude: halve the tick interval, drop “hours” language, and size verify effort as a ratio of observed build time, never a fixed hour.

title: Spec the click, not just the view
body: Every view row in a plan carries one more column: on record click → what. An empty cell is a decision that must be made before build, not after the owner asks. This is or001's “spec states, not screens” applied to interactions.

title: Silence the wolf
body: Watchdog suppresses “gone quiet” for agents with no open mission, and alert classes tied to a fixed bug retire with the fix. Twelve unactionable alerts per four-hour window is how the thirteenth, real one, gets skimmed past.

title: File the lesson while it stings
body: The moment a probe lands, the lesson goes into learnings.jsonl with the probe as its evidence ref — same turn as the apology. A lesson that lives only in chat is repeated; one that lives in the store is loaded into the next chief's onboarding.

html: Same operation as or001, one addition. The chief runs on Kimi wire — wire-tracer.py (BASE-overridable copy) produced the digest, stats, and greps; the builder runs Claude Code, so its session JSONL was parsed directly — same idea, different schema: tool_use blocks instead of loop events. Every handoff timestamp was cross-checked against the tunnel journal. Claims were verified against the round-1 commit (git show 5ad6bf7 — the inspector census), the live server (mutation probe), and the chief's own screenshots. The new technique worth keeping: the before/after-click screenshot pair — two identical frames are the whole proof that a click does nothing. And because the reviewed session was still alive and growing, wires were copied to scratchpad before reading — trace the copy, never the live file.

This is genuinely exciting for Novakai — Kimi's first complete run as chief shipped working software, five times faster than Friday's marathon. The method held under a real mission. The one flaw that slipped through is the already-named one from or001, a single question from Chris surfaced it in six minutes, and round 2 was fixing it within the hour. Feel good about this one.
